key_questions: Fagerstrom Test for Nicotine Dependence: time to first cigarette after waking (<30 min = high dependence)?
Number of cigarettes per day (>20/day = high dependence)?
Previous quit attempts: what helped, what caused relapse?
Motivation to quit (stages of change: precontemplation, contemplation, preparation, action)?
Comorbid depression, anxiety, or other mental health conditions?
red_flags: Active cardiovascular disease: NRT is SAFE (nicotine without carcinogens is far safer than smoking) — do NOT withhold NRT
Pregnancy: NRT safe; varenicline/bupropion — discuss risk-benefit
Severe psychiatric illness: varenicline is safe (BBW was overestimated — EAGLES trial); address psychiatric stability
Current medications affected by smoking cessation: clozapine/theophylline levels rise dramatically when patient stops smoking (CYP1A2 induction reduced)

first_line.name: Varenicline (Champix)
first_line.drugs: Varenicline (Champix): Week 1: 0.5 mg OD × 3 days → 0.5 mg BID × 4 days → Week 2+: 1 mg BID × 12 weeks
first_line.notes: Most effective single cessation agent (NNT ~7). Partial nicotinic receptor agonist — reduces craving AND blocks reward. Take with food (reduces nausea). Set quit date at Day 8–14. Pharmacist can prescribe under Ontario minor ailments. EAGLES trial: neuropsychiatric risk lower than BBW suggested — safe in stable psychiatric patients.
first_line.preferred_when: Most patients — most effective; also for patients with prior NRT failure

first_line.name: Combination NRT
first_line.drugs: Nicotine patch 14–21 mg (based on >/<10 cigarettes/day) + PRN short-acting (gum 2–4 mg, lozenge 2–4 mg, or inhaler)
first_line.notes: Combination NRT (patch + short-acting) superior to monotherapy. Long-acting patch for baseline; short-acting PRN for breakthrough cravings. Pharmacist can prescribe. Safe in cardiovascular disease.
first_line.preferred_when: Prefer non-prescription or prior varenicline failure; pregnancy (NRT > pharmacotherapy)

second_line.name: Bupropion SR (Zyban)
second_line.drugs: Bupropion SR (Zyban) 150 mg OD × 3 days then 150 mg BID (at least 8h apart) × 7–12 weeks — start 1–2 weeks before quit date
second_line.notes: Cessation rate 2.0× placebo. Useful if comorbid depression. Avoid in seizure disorder, eating disorders, alcohol withdrawal. Pharmacist can prescribe under Ontario minor ailments.

non_pharm: Smokers Helpline: 1-877-513-5333 (free Ontario service — coaches, text support)
Identify and plan for triggers (coffee, alcohol, stress, after meals)
Remove all cigarettes, lighters, ashtrays from home/car
Behavioral strategies: delay, distraction, deep breathing, drink water
Weight gain (4–5 kg average): counsel proactively — exercise, diet strategies
Average 8–14 quit attempts before sustained abstinence — every attempt builds skill
treatment.refer_when: Complex psychiatric comorbidity requiring specialist management; pregnancy (specialist input); significant cardiovascular disease (most can be managed by pharmacist with NRT); withdrawal seizure risk (alcohol or BZD co-dependence)
